The **2011 Saskatchewan general election** was held on November 7, 2011, to elect 58 members of the [Legislative Assembly of Saskatchewan](/wiki/Legislative_Assembly_of_Saskatchewan "Legislative Assembly of Saskatchewan") (MLAs).[[2]](#cite_note-2) The election was called on October 10 by the [Lieutenant Governor of Saskatchewan](/wiki/Lieutenant_Governor_of_Saskatchewan "Lieutenant Governor of Saskatchewan"), on the advice of [Premier](/wiki/Premier_of_Saskatchewan "Premier of Saskatchewan") [Brad Wall](/wiki/Brad_Wall "Brad Wall").[[3]](#cite_note-LGBarnhart-3) Wall's [Saskatchewan Party](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Party "Saskatchewan Party") government was re-elected with an increased majority of 49 seats, the third-largest majority government in the province's history. The opposition [New Democratic Party](/wiki/Saskatchewan_New_Democratic_Party "Saskatchewan New Democratic Party") was cut down to only nine ridings, its worst showing in almost 30 years.

This was the first Saskatchewan provincial vote to use a fixed election date, set on the first Monday of November every four years.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## Results

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2011_Saskatchewan_general_election&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Results")]

On election night, the incumbent Saskatchewan Party won 84% of the seats in the provincial legislature on the strength of 64% of the popular vote. In the process, they won the third-biggest majority government (in terms of percentage of seats won) in the province's history. The only bigger majorities came in [1934](/wiki/1934_Saskatchewan_general_election "1934 Saskatchewan general election"), when the Liberals won 50 out of 55 seats, and 1982, when the Tories won 55 out of 64. The NDP recorded its lowest share of the popular vote since [1938](/wiki/1938_Saskatchewan_general_election "1938 Saskatchewan general election"), when it was known as the Saskatchewan [Co-operative Commonwealth Federation](/wiki/Co-operative_Commonwealth_Federation "Co-operative Commonwealth Federation"). The NDP was reduced to its smallest presence in the legislature since [1982](/wiki/1982_Saskatchewan_general_election "1982 Saskatchewan general election"), when the party won the same number of seats in what was then a larger assembly. Opposition leader [Dwain Lingenfelter](/wiki/Dwain_Lingenfelter "Dwain Lingenfelter") was unseated.

The Saskatchewan Party maintained their dominance of rural regions, and also broke the NDP's longstanding grip on the province's two largest cities, [Regina](/wiki/Regina,_Saskatchewan "Regina, Saskatchewan") and [Saskatoon](/wiki/Saskatoon "Saskatoon"). The [Green Party](/wiki/Green_Party_of_Saskatchewan "Green Party of Saskatchewan") failed to win any seats – though they ran a full slate of 58 candidates and took third place in the overall popular vote, ahead of the [Liberal Party](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Liberal_Party "Saskatchewan Liberal Party"). The Liberals put most of their resources into getting party leader Ryan Bater elected in the Battlefords, but he finished a distant third. The [Progressive Conservatives](/wiki/Progressive_Conservative_Party_of_Saskatchewan "Progressive Conservative Party of Saskatchewan") made a small gain in popular vote for the second straight election.

### Results by region

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2011_Saskatchewan_general_election&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Results by region")]

The Saskatchewan Party maintained their sweep of the southern and central rural ridings. The Saskatchewan Party succeeded in unseating New Democrats in all of the smaller cities – including [Moose Jaw](/wiki/Moose_Jaw "Moose Jaw"), [The Battlefords](/wiki/The_Battlefords_(provincial_electoral_district) "The Battlefords (provincial electoral district)"), and [Prince Albert](/wiki/Prince_Albert,_Saskatchewan "Prince Albert, Saskatchewan"). The Saskatchewan Party also won eight of the 12 ridings in Saskatoon, marking the first time since the 1982 PC landslide that a [centre-right](/wiki/Canadian_conservatism "Canadian conservatism") party had won the most seats in that city. This didn't come as a surprise, since Saskatoon has traditionally been friendly to centre-right parties and candidates. However – and perhaps most surprisingly – the Saskatchewan Party also took eight out of 11 ridings in Regina, in part due to picking up local support from the largely absent Liberal Party. As was the case in Saskatoon, this was the first time a centre-right party had won the most seats there since 1982.

The New Democratic Party maintained their hold on the [two northernmost ridings](/wiki/Northern_Saskatchewan "Northern Saskatchewan") in Saskatchewan, in addition to three seats in the provincial capital and four constituencies in Saskatoon. The NDP recorded the lowest share of the popular vote since 1938 (when it was known as the CCF). However, compared to its result in 1982, NDP support in 2011 was more concentrated in the North and the inner cities of Regina and Saskatoon, a factor which allowed the party to equal its 1982-seat tally (and indeed exceed it in terms of proportion of seats). Also, for the first time in history, a Saskatchewan NDP leader lost his own seat, with [Dwain Lingenfelter](/wiki/Dwain_Lingenfelter "Dwain Lingenfelter") losing by a shocking 10-percentage-point margin in [Regina Douglas Park](/wiki/Regina_Douglas_Park "Regina Douglas Park") to a Saskatchewan Party challenger.

## Timeline

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2011_Saskatchewan_general_election&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Timeline")]

### 2007

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2011_Saskatchewan_general_election&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2007")]

* November 21, 2007 – [Premier](/wiki/Premier_of_Saskatchewan "Premier of Saskatchewan") [Brad Wall](/wiki/Brad_Wall "Brad Wall") & [Cabinet Ministers](/wiki/Executive_Council_of_Saskatchewan "Executive Council of Saskatchewan") are sworn in.

### 2008

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2011_Saskatchewan_general_election&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2008")]

* January 3, 2008 – [NDP](/wiki/Saskatchewan_New_Democratic_Party "Saskatchewan New Democratic Party") [MLA](/wiki/Legislative_Assembly_of_Saskatchewan "Legislative Assembly of Saskatchewan") [Joan Beatty](/wiki/Joan_Beatty "Joan Beatty") announces she will resign her seat in [Cumberland](/wiki/Cumberland_(Saskatchewan_provincial_electoral_district) "Cumberland (Saskatchewan provincial electoral district)") to enter federal politics.
* June 25, 2008 – [Doyle Vermette](/wiki/Doyle_Vermette "Doyle Vermette") holds the seat of [Cumberland](/wiki/Cumberland_(Saskatchewan_provincial_electoral_district) "Cumberland (Saskatchewan provincial electoral district)") for the [New Democrats](/wiki/Saskatchewan_New_Democratic_Party "Saskatchewan New Democratic Party").
* October 16, 2008 – [NDP](/wiki/Saskatchewan_New_Democratic_Party "Saskatchewan New Democratic Party") Leader [Lorne Calvert](/wiki/Lorne_Calvert "Lorne Calvert") announces he will retire from politics as soon as his successor is chosen.

### 2009

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2011_Saskatchewan_general_election&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 2009")]

* May 29, 2009 – [Premier](/wiki/Premier_of_Saskatchewan "Premier of Saskatchewan") [Brad Wall](/wiki/Brad_Wall "Brad Wall") shuffles his [Cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_(government) "Cabinet (government)").
* June 6, 2009 – [Dwain Lingenfelter](/wiki/Dwain_Lingenfelter "Dwain Lingenfelter") is elected Leader of the [Saskatchewan NDP](/wiki/Saskatchewan_New_Democratic_Party "Saskatchewan New Democratic Party") over [Ryan Meili](/wiki/Ryan_Meili "Ryan Meili") on the second ballot.
* June 30, 2009 – [NDP](/wiki/Saskatchewan_New_Democratic_Party "Saskatchewan New Democratic Party") [MLA](/wiki/Legislative_Assembly_of_Saskatchewan "Legislative Assembly of Saskatchewan") [Harry Van Mulligen](/wiki/Harry_Van_Mulligen "Harry Van Mulligen") resigns his seat in [Regina Douglas Park](/wiki/Regina_Douglas_Park "Regina Douglas Park"), officially retiring from politics. [Lorne Calvert](/wiki/Lorne_Calvert "Lorne Calvert") resigns his seat in [Saskatoon Riversdale](/wiki/Saskatoon_Riversdale "Saskatoon Riversdale") the same day.
* September 21, 2009 – [Dwain Lingenfelter](/wiki/Dwain_Lingenfelter "Dwain Lingenfelter") & [Danielle Chartier](/wiki/Danielle_Chartier "Danielle Chartier") hold the constituencies of [Regina Douglas Park](/wiki/Regina_Douglas_Park "Regina Douglas Park") & [Saskatoon Riversdale](/wiki/Saskatoon_Riversdale "Saskatoon Riversdale"), respectively, for the [NDP](/wiki/Saskatchewan_New_Democratic_Party "Saskatchewan New Democratic Party").
* October 21, 2009 – [Dwain Lingenfelter](/wiki/Dwain_Lingenfelter "Dwain Lingenfelter") is sworn in as [Leader of the Official Opposition](/wiki/Leader_of_the_Opposition_(Saskatchewan) "Leader of the Opposition (Saskatchewan)").

### 2010

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2011_Saskatchewan_general_election&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 2010")]

* January 29, 2010 – [NDP](/wiki/Saskatchewan_New_Democratic_Party "Saskatchewan New Democratic Party") [MLA](/wiki/Legislative_Assembly_of_Saskatchewan "Legislative Assembly of Saskatchewan") [Kim Trew](/wiki/Kim_Trew "Kim Trew") announces that he will not be running in the next election.[[5]](#cite_note-Trew-5)
* April 16, 2010 – [Saskatchewan Party](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Party "Saskatchewan Party") [MLA](/wiki/Legislative_Assembly_of_Saskatchewan "Legislative Assembly of Saskatchewan") [Serge LeClerc](/wiki/Serge_LeClerc "Serge LeClerc") resigns from the caucus to sit as an Independent MLA.[[6]](#cite_note-6) On April 20, he announces that he will not be running in the next election.[[7]](#cite_note-7)
* May 13, 2010 – [NDP](/wiki/Saskatchewan_New_Democratic_Party "Saskatchewan New Democratic Party") [MLA](/wiki/Legislative_Assembly_of_Saskatchewan "Legislative Assembly of Saskatchewan") [Ron Harper](/wiki/Ron_Harper_(politician) "Ron Harper (politician)") announces that he will retire at the end of his term.[[8]](#cite_note-Ron-Harper-8)
* June 2, 2010 – [Saskatchewan Party](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Party "Saskatchewan Party") [MLA](/wiki/Legislative_Assembly_of_Saskatchewan "Legislative Assembly of Saskatchewan") [Joceline Schriemer](/wiki/Joceline_Schriemer "Joceline Schriemer") announces that she will not run for re-election.[[9]](#cite_note-Schriemer-9)
* June 23, 2010 – [Finance Minister](/wiki/Minister_of_Finance "Minister of Finance") [Rod Gantefoer](/wiki/Rod_Gantefoer "Rod Gantefoer") announces that he will retire at the next election.[[10]](#cite_note-Gantefoer-10)
* June 29, 2010 – [Premier](/wiki/Premier_of_Saskatchewan "Premier of Saskatchewan") [Brad Wall](/wiki/Brad_Wall "Brad Wall") shuffles his [Cabinet](/wiki/Executive_Council_of_Saskatchewan "Executive Council of Saskatchewan").[[11]](#cite_note-11)
* August 31, 2010 – [Independent](/wiki/Independent_(politician)#Canada "Independent (politician)") [MLA](/wiki/Legislative_Assembly_of_Saskatchewan "Legislative Assembly of Saskatchewan") [Serge LeClerc](/wiki/Serge_LeClerc "Serge LeClerc") resigns his seat in the Legislature, leaving politics.[[12]](#cite_note-12)
* October 18, 2010 – [Gordon Wyant](/wiki/Gordon_Wyant "Gordon Wyant") of the [Saskatchewan Party](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Party "Saskatchewan Party") wins the seat of [Saskatoon Northwest](/wiki/Saskatoon_Northwest "Saskatoon Northwest") in a byelection.[[13]](#cite_note-Wyant-byelection-13)

### 2011

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2011_Saskatchewan_general_election&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: 2011")]

* January 11, 2011 – [NDP](/wiki/Saskatchewan_New_Democratic_Party "Saskatchewan New Democratic Party") [MLA](/wiki/Legislative_Assembly_of_Saskatchewan "Legislative Assembly of Saskatchewan") [Pat Atkinson](/wiki/Pat_Atkinson "Pat Atkinson") announces that she will retire at the next provincial election.[[14]](#cite_note-Atkinson-14)
* March 5, 2011 – [Saskatchewan Party](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Party "Saskatchewan Party") [MLA](/wiki/Legislative_Assembly_of_Saskatchewan "Legislative Assembly of Saskatchewan") [Denis Allchurch](/wiki/Denis_Allchurch "Denis Allchurch") loses his party's nomination for [Rosthern-Shellbrook](/wiki/Rosthern-Shellbrook "Rosthern-Shellbrook") to [Scott Moe](/wiki/Scott_Moe "Scott Moe").[[15]](#cite_note-Allchurch-15)
* September 6, 2011 – Larissa Shasko abruptly resigns from the leadership of the [Green Party of Saskatchewan](/wiki/Green_Party_of_Saskatchewan "Green Party of Saskatchewan"); Shasko also gives up her candidacy for the Greens in [Moose Jaw North](/wiki/Moose_Jaw_North "Moose Jaw North").[[16]](#cite_note-Green-Resign-16) Federal [Green Party of Canada](/wiki/Green_Party_of_Canada "Green Party of Canada") leader [Elizabeth May](/wiki/Elizabeth_May "Elizabeth May") announces (via Twitter) that veteran provincial party activist [Victor Lau](/w/index.php?title=Victor_Lau&action=edit&redlink=1 "Victor Lau (page does not exist)") will temporarily lead the Saskatchewan Greens.[[16]](#cite_note-Green-Resign-16)
* September 25, 2011 – Lau elected leader of the [Green Party](/wiki/Green_Party_of_Saskatchewan "Green Party of Saskatchewan") at an [extraordinary convention](/wiki/Extraordinary_General_Meeting "Extraordinary General Meeting") in Regina.[[17]](#cite_note-Green-Convention-17)
* October 10, 2011 – Premier Brad Wall asks [Lieutenant Governor](/wiki/Lieutenant_Governor_of_Saskatchewan "Lieutenant Governor of Saskatchewan") [Gordon Barnhart](/wiki/Gordon_Barnhart "Gordon Barnhart") to dissolve the [Legislative Assembly](/wiki/26th_Legislative_Assembly_of_Saskatchewan "26th Legislative Assembly of Saskatchewan") and issue [writs of election](/wiki/Writ_of_election "Writ of election").[[3]](#cite_note-LGBarnhart-3)
* October 22, 2011 – Nominations close with 191 candidates running in 58 electoral districts.[[18]](#cite_note-18)
